summary: This post examines whether developer compensation is becoming bimodal like it has in law and consulting, where income distributions show two distinct peaks rather than a smooth distribution. While fields like law have clear bimodal patterns driven by tournament-like up-or-out structures and lockstep pay scales, programmer compensation appears to have high dispersion but lacks the sharp separation into distinct modes. The author struggles to find a convincing explanation for why programmers are paid so well compared to other engineering fields that require similar skills, noting that common explanations like 'software is eating the world' or 'programming is hard' apply equally to fields that pay much less.
key_points: Law and consulting have developed bimodal compensation distributions over time, with a hollowing out of the middle
Limited data on programmer total compensation (including stock/bonus) makes it hard to determine if the field is truly bimodal
Programming lacks the typical barriers to high pay seen in other fields: no prestige filter, no licensing cartel, no tournament structure, and doesn't require specific credentials
High programmer pay appears driven by specific market dynamics (FB breaking wage-fixing agreements) rather than inherent properties of the field

Common explanations for high programmer pay (importance of software, difficulty, productivity multipliers) fail to explain why programming pays more than other engineering fields where the same logic applies
money_quotes: Because the mechanism for compensation increases has been arbitrary (FB could not exist, or the person who's in total control of FB, Zuckerberg, could decide on different compensation policy), it's quite arbitrary that programmer pay is as good as it is.

If their filters were perfect (as opposed to the complete joke that they're widely regarded to be), they couldn't be better than 90%-ile. Realistically, it's more likely that the median programmer at a high-paying tech company is a bit above 50%-ile.
The thing all of these theories have in common is that they apply to other fields as well, so they cannot be, as stated, the reason programmers are better paid than people in these other fields.

Any argument of the form, 'the price for X is high because X is important' implicitly assumes that there's some force constraining the supply of X. The claim that 'X is important' or 'we need a lot of X' is missing half the story.
Google alone employs 30k engineers. If 20k are programmers in the U.S. and the estimate that there are 3 million programmers in the U.S., Google alone employs 0.6% of programmers in the U.S.
key_insight: High programmer compensation appears to be driven by contingent historical factors like the collapse of wage-fixing agreements rather than inherent properties of the field, and common explanations fail because they apply equally to lower-paid engineering disciplines.
